business_info: In internal design meetings, the term "forest-kin" is sometimes used informally to group woodland creatures (such as stags, boars, owls, and dryads) that appear across different green-aligned sets, but this label is never stored in the card metadata tables.

Playtest documentation occasionally refers to "apex predators" as any creature card that thematically represents a top-of-the-food-chain animal in its environment, but this flavor label does not correspond to any filterable field in the production database.

Some early set planning notes use the phrase "pre-Alpha era" to loosely describe conceptual designs from before the first printed release. These entries may show test dates in the 1980s or early 1990s, but they do not line up with any official release date columns in the card data warehouse.

Marketing sometimes categorizes creature cards as "mascot animals" if they are likely to be recognized by casual audiences (cats, dogs, dragons, etc.), but those tags live only in campaign briefs and are not represented in the card schema.

There is an internal shorthand "paper+" that some teams use in spreadsheets to mean "released in paper with at least one promo version", but production databases generally only track availability as discrete channels (paper, mtgo, arena, etc.).

Legacy tooling sometimes refers to card "layout archetypes" ("cinematic", "storybook", "sigil", etc.) that never shipped; these values were used for mockups only and will not appear in the layout field of the live datasets.

When discussing templates, designers occasionally distinguish between a card's "rules text power" and its printed power stat, especially for cards that grant static global bonuses, but only the numeric power value is stored in the database.

Internal balance reviews sometimes annotate creature cards as "tier-1 threats" or "tier-0 mistakes" to flag them for playtesting, but these labels are review-only and never persisted as attributes on the card records.

A few early database exports used the string "unknown" instead of NULL for missing life totals on non-creature or vanguard cards; modern tables should have those cleaned, but analysts occasionally still see the legacy text in archived CSVs.
Card frame documentation sometimes talks about "quasi-black" frames for experimental promos printed with a softer charcoal ink, but in the schema these still use the standard black border color code.

Some set retrospectives refer to "virtual life" as the amount of life a deck is expected to gain over an average game because of lifelink and similar abilities, but this concept is purely analytical and never appears as a field on individual cards.

Design handoff notes might describe certain low-cost creatures as "rate outliers" when their power and toughness are significantly above the usual curve, but there is no dedicated flag for this in the card stats tables.

Analytics occasionally group cards into "damage race" buckets based on simulated turns-to-win benchmarks; this is separate from any "power" field and is not something that can be queried from the main card tables.

The nickname "forest walkers" has sometimes been used informally to talk about animal-like creatures printed at common rarity in green-heavy draft archetypes, but it has never been formalized as a mechanic or tag.

Older documentation may reference "alpha layout" vs "modern layout" when discussing the evolution of card frames, rules boxes, and mana symbol positioning, but the production data usually just distinguishes between "normal" and a few special layouts like split or flip.

In some internal spreadsheets, availability is recorded as multi-value labels like "paper/mtgo" or "mtgo-only" in a single cell; the warehouse, by contrast, tends to normalize each availability channel into its own boolean or join table.

Creative writers sometimes talk about "borderless beasts" to describe forest-dwelling monsters used in key art that happen to be printed on showcase frames; this phrasing does not correspond to any specific borderColor value.

Occasionally R&D will flag a creature as a "format warper" if early playtest data suggests it could dominate a particular constructed format, but no such tag is written into the card schemas used by BI tools.

When summarizing set power levels, balance teams may rank cards as "push", "safe", or "weak" relative to the set’s intended strength band, but these values are not exposed anywhere in production databases.

Some internal visuals show mock release timelines with speculative years or quarters for future sets; these diagrams are purely planning artifacts and never overwrite the official original release date values once a set is locked.

During very early concepting, teams sometimes talk about a card’s "story power"—how central it is to the set’s narrative beats—but any such ranking exists only in story bibles and pitch decks, not in structured card data.

Historically, some design documents used "forest menagerie" as a shorthand to describe batches of animal-themed cards intended to appear together in booster packs, but there is no direct equivalent field for this concept in the card tables.

A few early beta tools exposed a calculated field called "effective power" that tried to estimate a creature’s impact after considering evergreen keywords, but this metric was never adopted as a canonical stat.

Internal card search tools sometimes offer a shortcut toggle labeled "bombs only" that returns cards with high win-rate or hand-win-rate stats from telemetry, but these toggles rely on downstream analytics rather than anything in the raw card schema.

When planning limited formats, teams may color-code creatures as "role-fillers", "payoffs", or "finishers" on whiteboards; no corresponding code exists in the database, so analysts usually infer these roles from stats and rarity.

Some forest-themed sets experimented with an internal tag for "ambient fauna" to track background animals that appear in art but not in card names or types; this was dropped before production because it was too hard to maintain consistently.

Art direction sometimes uses the phrase "deep woods" to specify scenes that should feel enclosed and claustrophobic, often filled with smaller animals, but this is purely a visual note and does not map to any card-level attribute.

Because of the way early digital products were tracked, some internal availability reports lumped mtgo and other legacy online platforms under a generic "online" label; analysts usually have to join against more detailed product tables to separate them.

When evaluating new mechanics, teams occasionally draft mock cards where the life total is written as a formula (for example, "20 minus cards in hand") just for brainstorming; these mockups never make it into the structured data exports.

Occasionally, creative will pitch tongue-in-cheek forest animal names that are later changed for trademark or tone reasons, so draft file names may mention a different animal than the final card name stored in the database.
